invariant_definition: Minute or atomic: a category of size and ontological minimality rather than a reference to modern physical atoms. In Vedānta the term often concerns the *jīva*, not gross matter. Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) insist that the individual self is *aṇu* so that embodiment, karmic individuation, and plurality are real. The reach of cognition is then explained not by the self's size but by a further apparatus: *dharmabhūta-jñāna* in Viśiṣṭādvaita, or divinely ordered cognition in Tattva-vāda. In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and related atomisms, by contrast, *aṇu* and *paramāṇu* name the smallest material constituents and the atomic *manas* that mediates episodic awareness. The same word thus spans debates about conscious-substance size and about physical constitution. It is best read together with *vibhu*, *parimāṇa*, *paramāṇu*, and the dispute over whether a self can be all-pervading while still having body-specific experience.

per_school.definition: For Advaita, *aṇutva* cannot belong to the *ātman* in its own nature, since Gauḍapāda presents the self as ākāśavat and denies that the *jīva* is either a real part or a real modification of it [[MK 3.3-7](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.3-7)]. The apparently differentiated *jīva* belongs to *māyā* and *upacāra*; the unborn reality does not truly become many, so minute measure has no *pāramārthika* purchase here [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21); [MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)].

In the canonized Yāmuna locus, the *ātman* is not described as *aṇu* but as *nitya*, *vyāpī*, and *pratikṣetra-bhinna*; individuation is thus secured without reducing the self to minute measure [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3)]. The indexed Rāmānuja passages accordingly place the weight on the self's distinction from body and on its ordered relation to the *antaryāmin*, not on intrinsic atomicity as the school's load-bearing account of *ātma-svarūpa* [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3); [VedS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4); [VedS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

The indexed Tattva-vāda loci make the decisive point through *bheda*: the *jīva* is irreducibly distinct from Īśvara and from other selves, and that plurality is real and beginningless within the *bhedapañcaka* [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Scriptural *abheda*-language is therefore taken as resting on *sādṛśya*, *prādhānya*, or dependent designation, never on *svarūpābhidā*; the self cannot be a vibhu Brahman under this hermeneutic [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)].

Jīva Gosvāmī explains the *jīva*-aggregate as standing to Bhagavān by his *svābhāvika* and *acintya-śakti* in the manner of the quality of his atomic rays, so that *abheda* must be read only together with real distinction [[SatS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. The minute status of the selves is thus subordinated to the *śakti-śaktimat* relation: they are conscious and dependent, neither sheer *svarūpaikya* nor sheer external otherness [[SatS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42); [SatS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)].

Nimbārka states the matter directly: the *jīva* is *aṇu*, *pratideha-bhinna*, possessed of *jñātṛtva*, and yet *hareḥ adhīna* [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. The school's *aṃśa*-reading keeps this minuteness within *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*: the self is really distinct, yet non-different as Brahman's dependent part and eternal emanational mode [[BS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42); [BS 2.3.43](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.43); [Gītā 15.7a](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/15.7a)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'atom.' Without qualification that invites modern physics and makes readers imagine a tiny material pellet. In classical usage *aṇu* is a size-category and often a metaphysical thesis about the self. Even where it does refer to material atoms, the issues are partlessness, combination, and causal constitution, not modern atomic theory.
